assumptions: The analysis concerns consolidated financial statements of a normal group, not the special case of an investment entity.

The intragroup dividend has already been declared such that a receivable/payable has been recognised; therefore the exposure is analysed as a recognised intragroup balance, not merely as a forecast transaction.
The receivable/payable is denominated in a currency different from at least one relevant entity's functional currency.
No additional facts indicate that the exposure is being designated as part of a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation.

approaches.label: General exclusion of intragroup items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: The default IFRS 9 rule is that only items with external parties can be designated as hedged items in consolidated financial statements. An intragroup dividend receivable is therefore not eligible unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: Start from a presumption of ineligibility in consolidated hedge accounting.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 says only exposures with parties external to the reporting entity qualify as hedged items in consolidated financial statements, subject to stated exceptions.

approaches.label: Foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 provides a specific exception for the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item in consolidated financial statements if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A recognised intragroup dividend receivable/payable may be analysed under this exception.
conditions: The recognised dividend receivable/payable is an intragroup monetary item
The entities involved have different functional currencies
The foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
IFRS 9 designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: Eligibility may exist, but only through the intragroup monetary item exception rather than the general rule.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when the FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and satisfaction of hedge effectiveness criteria.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction exception
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 separately permits certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged when their FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss. That route does not fit well once a dividend receivable has already been recognised.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not rely on the forecast-transaction exception for a dividend exposure that is already booked as a receivable/payable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 6.3.6 and 6.3.7 allow highly probable forecast intragroup transactions to qualify only if denominated in another currency and their FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraphs B6.3.5 and B6.3.6 stress that many intragroup flows do not qualify unless they affect consolidated profit or loss; qualifying cash flow hedge effects are recognised through OCI.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge model
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 deals with hedges of a net investment in a foreign operation, where the hedged item is an amount of net assets of the foreign operation. It is a distinct model and does not itself make an intragroup dividend receivable eligible.
approaches.practical_implication: If the risk management objective is foreign operation net investment risk, designate the net investment exposure, not the dividend receivable itself.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 10-14 identify the hedged risk and hedged item in a net investment hedge as foreign currency risk on an amount of net assets of the foreign operation.
Paragraph 8 says the Interpretation applies only to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and should not be applied by analogy to other hedge accounting questions.
